The returns for the FlexShares ETF are weighed down by higher fees. IQDF charges a management fee of 0.47%. This is lower than the median ETF, but higher than most other passively managed funds. VYMI, as one might expect from a Vanguard fund, has an expense ratio of just 0.17%, leaving more gains for its shareholders. Higher fees paired with lower returns certainly decreases the attractiveness of IQDF.

Risks to Consider

According to the fund’s Fact Sheet, the management fees for this ETF may increase next year. The fund’s advisor, Northern Trust Investments, has agreed to reimburse a portion of the operating expenses from IQDF in order to keep the expense ratio at 0.47%. However, this agreement expires on March 1, 2026. The Fact Sheet states that without this fee waiver in place, previous fund results would have been lower than what was stated.

One added contrast between IQDF and VYMI to consider is the composition of each portfolio with regards to the countries in which each fund invests. While the difference is not a massive one, IQDF relies on more returns from companies in emerging markets. The chart below from ETF.com shows this difference and points to the potential for added risk from the portfolio from the FlexShares ETF.

Even though international funds have had a strong year, there is no guarantee that they will continue to generate higher returns in the next 12 months. Over the long term, US equities have proven to be the best place for investors to put their money. IQDF, like any fund that invests in securities outside the US, should only be used for diversification purposes and should not represent a majority allocation in any investor’s portfolio.

The Bottom Line

IQDF receives a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant system as the fund gets high marks in both Dividends and Risk. The Dividends score should come as no surprise. The Risk score is an "A" because the fund follows its benchmark so well over the last one-, three-, and five-year periods. The fund's relatively high management fees impact its grade for Expenses.

For investors who are almost completely focused on dividend yield, the FlexShares International Quality Dividend Index Fund ETF may be worth a longer look. However, for investors who are looking for total return, there appear to be better options. I agree with the Seeking Alpha Quant system and deem IQDF to be a Hold.

data.title: Rio Tinto: Restructuring And Growing In Future-Facing Metals
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO), I called them an “Iron Mining Gem At Attractive Prices, With Excellent Dividends Plus Growth Potential” thanks to their strong financials, healthy margins, and a robust balance sheet and yield that comes with good growth potential thanks to their exposure to copper and lithium, on top of iron’s stability and reliability.

The stock is basically flat since covering them back in July, although there have been several internal and external developments that I believe are worth looking into.

Recent Quarter

The company has since then reported its H1 ’25, reporting a solid increase in copper and bauxite production, which offset the 13% reduction in ore prices. Based on the $6.9 billion operating cash flow, iron ore represented 55% of it (vs. 72% in H1 ’24), while aluminum grew to 23%, copper to 19%, and minerals fell to 2% (I’ll mention these later as they are very important).

Still, despite the fall in iron ore prices, the net cash generated from operating activities (operating cash flow) is down by only 2%, although the net debt is up significantly to $14.6 billion (of which $7.6 billion was associated with the acquisition of Arcadium Lithium, closed in March 2025). But this is still their weakest H1 profit in 5 years, and with falling iron ore prices and tariff uncertainty, I would’ve expected a much worse reaction from the market.

As we can see, the free cash flow fell 39% due to the higher CAPEX combined especially with the lower revenue, but it’s still a relatively solid number. This comes during an environment where the company is affected by macro headwinds, but an annualized ~$6.1 billion even with 15% higher CAPEX is still not terrible for a $108.8 billion market cap, especially for average and below-average commodity prices.

Another internal development is the company’s announcement of a restructuring plan, which includes a three-division structure to drive the next growth phase, with new management and three new pillars (iron, aluminum + lithium, and copper) and a new CEO since late August 2025. They say that they’ll also focus on reviewing the strategic importance of their non-core assets (e.g. borates, titanium, etc.), with the goal of streamlining the operations and allowing them to channel their capital towards future-facing commodities.

Also, we’ve recently seen an appeals court ruling that temporarily halted a land transfer essential for Rio and BHP (BHP) to advance the Resolution Copper project in Arizona, which is a massive copper project that can provide about 25% of the US’ current copper demand, prompting Trump to criticize the ruling.

Macro Changes

Of course, commodities remained very volatile (even if we exclude lithium), with iron down but offset a bit by copper and aluminum. Combined with the stable operating cash flow, this shows good resilience from Rio, and we have to keep in mind that, as they say, copper and aluminum are trading around long-term averages, while iron and lithium are still trading well below that, meaning that an upcycle can come with significant upside potential, while the below-average levels we see now still result in solid results.

Tariffs also play a very important role in this company’s performance, as the US (and especially China, which is targeted by these tariffs) represents a massive player when it comes to iron and steel. Even if Rio were to not be affected directly by the tariffs, anything that can slow down the global economy (or even just China) can hurt the price of iron, as the country accounts for even more than 50% of the global iron ore consumption (used for steel and industry), while accounting for even ~75% of seaborne trade of the commodity, while copper and aluminum numbers aren’t far from this either.

The 50% aluminum tariffs did hurt them directly, however, with Rio estimating that tariffs on their Canadian-made aluminum resulted in gross costs of $321M in H1’25 alone, which is massive considering how it didn’t even affect the entire 6-month period.

As I said in the initial analysis, US rate cuts are one of the main near-term catalysts. These can have significant effects on the price and demand of all the metals that Rio is producing, and now with two more cuts expected this year after the one in September, we could be looking at a rapidly improving environment. This is because rate cuts can lead to a weaker dollar, especially if we pair it with the significant uncertainty around tariffs and politics in general and fear around a hard landing or even a recession in the US. As a note, the latter would be bad for Rio’s commodity, as virtually everything they produce would be bad during further economic downturns.

Regarding the Arcadium Lithium deal, back when I looked into the broader lithium industry, this was one of the best investments out there, with significant potential following the merger that created them, with a very solid organic growth pipeline that allowed them to grow production even 3-4 times. At the time, this would’ve made Arcadium the third-largest lithium producer globally, which means Rio Tinto now has very good chances of fighting lithium giants like SQM (SQM) or Albemarle (ALB), with significantly more capital available that they can use to expand, while companies like Albemarle (which depend on lithium prices) can’t afford to invest in a downcycle. In this regard, Rio Tinto’s timing was excellent, in my opinion, buying when even the industry’s giants are struggling, highlighting why this type of production diversification can be important.

Also, a few days ago we saw Lithium Americas (LAC) more than double following reports of the US government seeking a stake in them, as the company is renegotiating terms of a $2.3 billion government loan while facing a potential cash crunch. This comes despite the administration’s views on lithium and its main long-term uses, as the supply-demand balance is expected to enter into a significant gap due to the low supply and investments, especially after the falling commodity prices forced producers to cut down their expansion plans. This is another reason why Rio’s move was great in my opinion, preparing the company for a massive potential upcycle while they got the potentially third-largest lithium company at a good price.

They also reportedly faced some pressure to build a refinery for their Simandou project, with the government of Guinea demanding that Rio establish downstream processing for the ore it produces rather than simply exporting raw materials abroad, which would ensure the country is able to capture some more value from it through jobs, local investment, etc. - which in turn would cost Rio some money at first.

Updated Valuation

I’d also like to adjust the valuation to their latest numbers, although keep in mind that the long-term potential throughout the cycle remains. Starting with an annualized $6.1 billion free cash flow based on H1’s CAPEX and OpCF in 2025, I’ll project a 3.75% annual growth for the first 5 years thanks to earlier rate cuts and potential for tariff uncertainty to diminish (note that they have a 3% CAGR production ambition from 2024 to 2033, which remains achievable thanks to their projects and deals). This is followed by 2.75% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years (slowing down due to their size, just to be safer), and I'll use a 2.25% terminal growth rate to represent the steady long-term demand for iron and the potential coming from copper and lithium and a 7.5% WACC (slightly above my estimated 7.39%). For their $14.6 billion net debt and 1.62 billion shares outstanding, this returns an enterprise value of $124.71 billion. After subtracting net debt, this gives an equity value of $110.11 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of ~$67.97, which is not bad even for relatively below-average levels.

Conclusions

Overall, Rio Tinto remains a buy for me, with the solid financials and long-term potential offsetting the volatility this industry comes with, plus a very solid dividend on top unlocking some value while I wait for better prices. The management changes and restructuring could offer a refreshed vision for their future steps, but until then we’re still likely to face some volatility due to tariffs and overall high political uncertainty, although the industry’s tailwinds remain solid in the long-term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Prosus: Tencent At A Discount, Plus A Growing E-Commerce Ecosystem For Free
data.description:
Prosus N.V. (OTCPK:PROSF) is a global investor in consumer internet companies, headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands. Prosus is known for its substantial stake in Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), dating back to an early investment made by its mother company, Naspers, in 2001. Tencent provides strong momentum with double-digit revenue growth and margin improvements. Aside from the Tencent investment, Prosus is on a mission to build a lifestyle e-commerce ecosystem in strongly growing markets. The ex-Tencent portfolio is more relevant than ever before as it is growing at high double-digit rates and has recently become profitable, marking an inflection point in Prosus' history.

Two major tailwinds matter, while Prosus quietly follows its mission to diversify and grow its portfolio into a synergistic ecosystem with complementary products. First, Tencent’s strong performance, and second, a persistent NAV discount, allowing Prosus to accrue value for shareholders by buying back its own shares with the proceeds from steady Tencent stake reduction. Prosus has a market cap of $147.2B and a NAV of $212.8B, representing a NAV discount of 30.8%. As the management team is incentivized to increase NAV/share, investors can expect the shareholder value-creating buyback program to continue.

My base case indicates expected returns of 17.6% per year. A potential future narrowing of the NAV discount provides further upside potential.

Prosus Mission

Prosus is on a mission to build an ecosystem of leading consumer internet companies across fast-growing markets. The countries, in which Prosus operates, typically have a young population with high GDP growth rates and strong consumption. On top of that, the markets offer a lot of growth potential, driven by high digital usage growth and relatively low mobile and internet penetration.

Prosus is focusing on adjacent sectors such as food delivery, payments & fintech, consumer services, and digital e-commerce. The idea behind this is simple: build a universe of companies and profit from synergy effects. Prosus leverages its global tech expertise by sharing infrastructure, knowledge, best practices, and connections. Providing its companies with important competitive advantages and helping them secure strong positions in their local market.

Prosus’ investments typically have a long-term horizon with plenty of room for growth. Prosus seeks to invest in promising companies, scale them with its expertise and realize their value either by sales, strategic partnerships, or acquisitions, thereby embedding them into its ecosystem.

Prosus specifically focuses on early-stage AI start-ups that have the potential to reshape the e-commerce value chain.

This includes shopping and advertising tech, to customer support, AI agents, and operations, all driving measurable impact and performance gains. (p. 39, FY25 Annual Report).

Prosus not only invests in AI but also applies it in its own operations. In 2022, Prosus rolled out its own agent-based AI assistant, Toqan, to more than 20,000 employees. Toqan is continuously adapting to Prosus' needs, making Prosus operations more efficient. Its automation leverages multiple large language models (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic) to support a wide range of daily tasks. It handles, for example, 95% of customer support, 90% of HR employee questions, connects and analyzes internal data, creates apps and much more.

Prosus is also developing its own Large Commerce Model (LCM). Unlike other models, LCM specializes in predicting user intent and actions on e-commerce platforms. To build it, Prosus uses high quality, open source models, reaching 20x lower costs and 5x faster performance compared to other models. Fed by billions of data patterns from its ecosystem, the model is increasingly becoming better at predicting user behavior and intent. This allows Prosus to improve user experience, marketing, conversion rates, and optimize many other operations across its platform.

Prosus' management is actively managing its portfolio with disciplined capital allocation and the primary goal of strengthening Prosus’ ecosystem. Stakes, which are considered non-strategic, are being reduced or sold, freeing up capital for new strategic investments in key regions.

Additionally, Prosus continually reduces its share count by repurchasing its own shares. These buybacks are funded by the sale of Tencent shares. As Prosus is trading at a significant discount to its NAV, this creates a value-accretive effect, boosting the NAV/Share.

The Tencent stake acts as a cash engine, enabling Prosus to repurchase its own discounted shares and finance its lucrative M&As. In the long run, investors can expect Prosus to become a globally diversified tech giant in up-trending markets, benefiting from economies of scale and AI efficiencies.

Prosus stake in Tencent

Tencent is a holding company that has created, over the years, a massive ecosystem of internet companies operating internationally and in China. The ecosystem includes consumer products, such as communication, social media, entertainment (games, music, videos), financial services, mobile utilities, and games. Most of these services are incorporated into its super app, Weixin/WeChat, which has a user base of 1.3 billion people. Tencent also offers business services, including marketing solutions, cloud/AI services, and other technologies for a wide range of industries.

Tencent recently posted strong quarterly results with double-digit revenue growth on a YoY basis across all segments. Total revenue grew by 15%, operating profit by 22%, and net income increased by 11% on a YoY basis. Over the last three years, Tencent was also able to grow its non-IFRS operating margin (operating margin adjusted for effects from M&A, share-based compensation, and tax) from 25.2% to 37.5%.

Tencent is currently trading at 20.8x FWD earnings, which represents a premium of 43% compared to the sector and a discount of 8% to its 5Y historical average.

Take note that Tencent is almost net debt-free and holds a large investment portfolio on its balance sheet. According to Tencent’s 2025 Interim Report, the fair value of its investments in listed and unlisted companies amounted to RMB 714.3 ($100.4B) and RMB 342.3 ($48.1B), for a total of RMB 1,056.6 ($148.5B). Meaning 20% of Tencent’s market cap are strategic investments. Adjusting Tencent’s FWD P/E by its investment portfolio results in a FWD P/E of only 16.7, much more in alignment with its sector.

Similar to Prosus’ mission, Tencent’s investments aim to strengthen and expand its existing ecosystem. With the help of its immense data on current market trends, Tencent is able to consistently identify new strategic high-potential companies. By investing in these companies, Tencent leverages its incomparable infrastructure and expertise to scale them, following its strategy to connect or vertically grow its own ecosystem. The investee companies profit from the access to Tencent’s universe, including user data, cloud services, payments, etc., gaining a competitive advantage.

Mentionable investments include JD.com (e-commerce), Meituan (food delivery), Epic Games, and Riot Games (international gaming). In the past, Tencent’s investment portfolio has generated significant fair value gains, IPO upbringings, and divestment proceeds. Tencent is also repurchasing its own shares (~1.5% p.a.) and paying a dividend (~0.7% p.a.).

Given the fact that Tencent is currently representing 80% of Prosus’ NAV, Prosus investors gain substantial exposure to this immense ecosystem, with a strong China focus, and therefore also to its specific geopolitical risks.

Prosus Ex-Tencent Ecosystem

As I have mentioned above, Prosus is on its mission to build its own universe of lifestyle e-commerce companies. Prosus recently acquired Just Eat, one of Europe’s biggest food delivery platforms, for $4.6B.

Adding this acquisition to its portfolio, the value of Ex-Tencent Assets amounts to $44.3B, representing the remaining 20% of Prosus’ NAV. This also includes Prosus Ventures, the group’s early-stage investment arm, worth $1.7B. Similar to Tencent, these ventures can be seen as win-win situations, as investee companies are benefiting from Prosus infrastructure and at the same time driving innovation across the Prosus ecosystem.

Prosus' own ecommerce portfolio is growing revenue organically at 18% CAGR over the last few years, while its operating margins are also rapidly improving. E-commerce revenue growth in local currency in FY25 was 21%, twice as much as its peers. The EBITDA margin almost doubled from 6% to 11%. Consequently, the Ex-Tencent Portfolio recently marked an inflection point, by being profitable and FCF positive for the first time. FY25 aEBIT was $443M and FCF $1B ($36M adjusted for Tencent Dividend).

Core Headline Earnings and Balance Sheet

Core Headline Earnings, a metric that tracks equity-accounted earnings (e.g. from Tencent and other associates), adjusted for non-operating items, one-time effects, and share-based compensation, grew from $5.0B to $7.4B, representing 59% growth per share on a YoY basis. This metric is important to follow, as it tracks the underlying operating profitability of both Prosus’ investment and its fully consolidated businesses.

Prosus is sitting on $16.6B cash and $15B debt, making the company net debt-free. Given this healthy balance sheet, Prosus can continue its expansion in the upcoming years. In the recent 12 months, Prosus invested $7.8B in M&A mainly for Just Eat and Despegar, Latin America's biggest local travel platform. While trimming stakes for $2.6B in Trip.com, Swiggy, and other stakes to further improve strategic and regional asset allocation (can also be read on p.17, FY 2025 Annual Report).

Despite the M&A activity, Prosus' balance sheet remains very healthy, allowing Prosus to continue its expansion in the upcoming years.

The pace of Prosus’ buybacks is unlikely to be affected, as they are funded by Tencent share sales and do not place a burden on Prosus' cash position.

Management Focus – Increase NAV/Share

With the installation of new CEO Fabricio Bloisi in July 2024, Prosus amplified its focus on operating its own profitable e-commerce portfolio as opposed to just investing in other companies.

Bloisi was previously the CEO of IFood, Brazil's biggest food delivery company, which is also nowadays part of the Prosus universe. Bloisi’s focus lies in improving the profitability of Prosus e-commerce platforms while enforcing strict cost discipline.

What shareholders should like about Prosus: Its management incentives are linked to long-term NAV/share growth.

The CEO is incentivized by Prosus’ Moonshot Program. A $100M bonus, which will be granted if Prosus and Naspers doubles its market cap by 2028 with above-average total returns compared to its peers.

At the same time, Prosus continues to simplify its company structure and repurchase its own shares at a pace of ~8 to 9% of all outstanding shares per year.

All these measures are strategically aligned. Leveraging its ecosystem to increase profitability and cut costs, create more transparency, and buy back its own shares at a discount, serve the shareholder-friendly goal to increase NAV per share.

Understanding the discounted Buyback Effect

This brings us to the buyback accretion effect. As I have mentioned, Prosus continuously buys back its own shares at discounts, recently between 30 and 40%. Meaning they only pay 60-70 cents from Tencent proceeds to buy 1 dollar of their own assets. In order to quantify the accrued value from the buybacks, following formula can be used.

To showcase the shareholder value-creating effect, I tried to use realistic numbers.

In 2024, Prosus repurchased 194,938,185 shares, translating to 7.8% of all outstanding shares. In 2025, so far, 155,881,443 shares were repurchased. By annualizing this number, I arrive at ~210M shares, translating into 9.1% of all outstanding shares and an average for the last 2 years of 8.5%.

Based on the public data of Prosus, the NAV discount average for the last 2 years was at 36%.

So by plugging in these values into the formula

x⋅d=0.085×0.36=0.0306

1−x=1−0.085=0.915

0.0306/0.915=3.34%

We receive an accretion to NAV/share of 3.34%.

The following chart shows the value accretion for different sets of NAV discounts and buyback rates.

The Tencent stake keeps growing in absolute numbers thanks to its strong underlying performance. At the same time, Prosus can use its ‘cheat code’ to generate 3 to 4% shareholder return per year. One can argue that Prosus is therefore quite comfortable with its heavy exposure to Tencent and the NAV discount coming with it, allowing the company to play the buyback game potentially for many more years.

Therefore, I believe Prosus’ management is doing a great job by slowly selling its Tencent stake without dragging Tencent’s share price down too much. And at the same time, not repurchasing its own shares too fast, making sure Prosus' share price isn’t growing too much, narrowing the NAV discount.

Over the next years, the NAV discount could narrow if Prosus continues to sell down its Tencent assets, thus reducing its weight in the NAV. In such a case, investors would profit, as it would mean higher share prices for them. For example, if the discount were to narrow or close from the current 30.8% to 20%, 10%, or 0%, investors would receive returns of 15.6%, 30.0%, and 44.5%.

Downside potential from a widening discount is limited, as an increase in the NAV discount would also increase the buyback accretion effect, partially offsetting the downside.

So, to sum it up, a persistent NAV discount coming from Prosus’ significant Tencent stake can actually be seen as a tailwind.

Valuation

Prosus has traded historically at a persistent discount – roughly between 15% and 45%, and in recent years, most of the time 30% to 40%. Meaning Prosus is historically trading at a discount to its Tencent stake, most of the time between 10 and 30%. Also meaning Prosus investors still gain access to the ex-Tencent portfolio, worth $44.3B for free!

My base case scenario for Prosus doesn’t assume a discount closing scenario. Instead, I want to focus on returns coming from NAV growth and accretion through the open-ended Share Repurchase Program.

Looking at Tencent’s EPS Estimates, and growth rates of 20.8%, 13.6%, and 13.2% for the next three years, I think it is fair to assume Prosus’ investment in Tencent is expected to grow at a CAGR of 13 to 14% over this time.

The ex-Tencent portfolio is growing revenues organically at roughly 18% per year in EUR terms, with even higher growth in local currencies. Combined with substantial margin expansion and the transformation into positive free cash flow in FY25, I believe a realistic expectation for its EPS growth lies between 15 and 20% per year.

This estimate refers to the consolidated e-commerce platforms such as OLX, iFood, PayU, and Despegar. The portfolio also includes equity stakes and early-stage ventures, which are not part of the organic growth figure. These provide potential upside, though their EPS contribution is harder to forecast.

By using a rather conservative 15% of annual NAV returns from the Ex-Tencent Portfolio, I arrive at combined expected yearly returns of 13.8% (0.8x13.5%+0.2x15%).

Compounding this growth together with the buyback accretion effect of around 3.3%, the total expected annual return increases to 17.6% in my base case scenario.

Keep in mind that this model is sensitive to changes in NAV discount, as I have described earlier. Although, keeping the positive developments of Prosus assets in mind, the ~30% NAV discount, appears historically fairly valued.

Tencent’s valuation is another sensitive variable. At today’s forward P/E of ~21x earnings, a re-rating to 24x would increase Tencent’s value by around 14%, which, given Tencent accounts for ~80% of Prosus’ NAV, translates into ~11% upside for Prosus shareholders. On the other hand, if Tencent were to de-rate to 18x, NAV would fall by around 14%, cutting Prosus’ NAV by around 11%. Translating into 3% to 4% more or less CAGR on a 3-year perspective, compared to my base case scenario.

All things considered, it is important to understand the mechanics, sensitivities, and uncertainties of my model.

To give some guidance in terms of a price target per share, I compounded my bear-, base- and bull case CAGRs over a 3-year horizon to receive 2028 price targets.

Bear Case (13% CAGR): 82.1 EUR ($96.44)

Base Case (17.6% CAGR): 92.5 EUR ($108.65)

Bull Case (21% CAGR): 100.8 EUR ($118.40)

Risks

One of the biggest risks remains the vast exposure to Tencent and thus to China and its regulatory environment. As explained above, the performance of Prosus is highly tied to the performance and valuation of Tencent.

The ex-Tencent portfolio is also heavily exposed to emerging markets, each coming with its own political and regulatory risks. These markets usually have weaker capital markets and higher currency fluctuations. As a result, investors tend to apply higher discount rates, which depresses the valuations of Prosus’ investments.

In addition, some parts of Prosus’ portfolio are still unprofitable, illiquid, or difficult to value. This increases the risk of future asset write-downs, which would reduce Prosus’ NAV.

Since Prosus is a conglomerate with a broad mix of investments in several regions and sectors, it is more difficult for investors to assess the value of its assets. The lack of transparency in segment reporting is adding up to this problem.

Lastly, as long as Tencent remains the biggest part of Prosus' NAV and China sentiment stays cautious, overall investor confidence in Prosus might remain subdued.

Conclusion

Prosus’ stake in Tencent acts as a major tailwind, providing strong growth, margins, investments, and shareholder returns. This enables Prosus to pursue its mission of building its own connected e-commerce ecosystem, driven by innovation, AI, and scale. Management is aligned with shareholders, as their number one goal is to increase NAV per share. Prosus is on a good path, recently reaching profitability in its ex-Tencent portfolio. My base case indicates a CAGR of 17.6% for the next 3 years. Despite the mentioned risks, I believe Prosus offers an asymmetric buying opportunity. The NAV discounts and buyback effect limit downside risk and give Prosus investors basically the ex-Tencent portfolio for free.

For international investors, Prosus also provides more direct access to Tencent compared to the Tencent ADR. For investors who do not have access to the direct listings of Prosus and Tencent. Prosus ADR (OTCPK:PROSY) should be safer than the Tencent ADR (OTCPK:TCEHY), as Prosus is EU-listed and not exposed to potential US-China ADR delisting risks.

For these reasons and the expected upside over the coming years, I assign Prosus a Buy rating.

data.title: British American Tobacco: Boring, Cheap, And Still Likely To Outperform
data.description:
Since my last article, covering by far the largest holding in my concentrated portfolio, British American Tobacco's (NYSE:BTI) share price has declined marginally by 2.48%.

In that article, I've downgraded the shares from a "Strong Buy" to a "Buy" based on valuation alone. The story behind the company hasn't changed, but since its lows in 2024 (at which time the shares were yielding 10%), the stock has gained more than 80%. The shares re-rated from just 6x P/FCF to a forward 12x P/FCF in just 16 months.

If you subscribe to the efficient market theory, there must have been new information available that drastically changed the trajectory of the company going forward. But there wasn't.

Sure, the U.S. business returned to growth for the first time since 2022, but it had been growing from 2018 to 2022, and the market didn't care, as the shares were trading at a single-digit P/E ratio with a near double-digit dividend yield most of the time.

The U.S. modern oral business has started to gain traction as Velo Plus was introduced, but the other two new categories saw declining revenues and volumes in both fiscal year 2024 and the first half of 2025.

I've been invested in British American Tobacco ever since it reached its lows in 2019. Over the following 5 years, I've added whenever the share price was in the lower part of its tight trading range. As the largest holding, I've been following the company closely. So, what has changed that has led to its breakout?

The reason for the great performance was its low starting valuation. At a time when most other consumer staple companies were trading at 20x to 30x P/FCF, BTI was trading around 8x P/FCF.

With a valuation this low, you'd expect a shrinking business, but that wasn't the case. At the time, BTI was growing in the low-to-mid single digits, which isn't unusual for a mature consumer staple company.

Going forward, I expect the future to be similar to the past. But given the higher valuation, I'm less excited about the stock than I was a year or two ago. I won't sell a share, but I'm not adding either.

If I were to discover BTI today, I'd be a buyer, but I wouldn't make it the largest holding in my portfolio. Valuation matters, especially when it comes to relatively slow-growing companies.

In my previous articles, I've talked extensively about the new generation products [NGP], and some of the new innovations have been launched recently. The main reason for this article is to update you on the progress.

But before we talk about the glo Hilo, Vuse Ultra, and Velo Shift, let's not forget that BTI's valuation is still relatively attractive, even though the shares have re-rated dramatically.

Valuation & Growth Rates

BTI is trading at an 11.4x forward P/E ratio. After the share price correction, the shares are yielding 6.14%. I know that most sources (and even analysts) state the dividend yield to be lower, but that's incorrect.

The original shares, trading under the ticker symbol BATS in London, are distributing GBP2.4024 a year, or GBP0.6006 a quarter, and those will get translated to USD once the quarterly dividend comes due. At current rates, it's a little over 6%.

Management aims to achieve 3% to 5% revenue growth and 4% to 6% adjusted profit from operations growth starting from 2026. That's a reasonable target since NGPs have started to contribute to profitability and the combustible business remains steady.

When it comes to free cash flow (FCF) generation, the latest target has been GBP50 billion of FCF before dividends between 2024 and 2030. If we annualize it, it comes down to GBP7.14 billion in FCF a year.

That's lower than in previous years (usually around GBP8 billion), and it doesn't add up with the mid-single-digit growth target mentioned before, unless the company is facing larger-than-usual exceptional items or significantly higher capital expenditures—investments in NGPs, for example.

Even if I don't quite understand the message behind the low FCF target, it doesn't worry me. British American Tobacco's (BATS in London) market cap is GBP85.8 billion right now.

GBP50 billion till 2030 is almost 60% of its market cap in just 6 years. In my opinion, that's still one of the best deals in the current exuberant market environment, where the average valuation has reached 30x P/E for the S&P 500.

The dividend consumes GBP5.26 billion a year, for a payout ratio of 74%, if the low target becomes a reality. While that may sound high, in the tobacco sector that's even slightly lower than Altria and Philip Morris, which distribute roughly 80% of their FCF to shareholders.

But let's forget about the management targets, and let's quickly do our own calculations. Let's assume BTI keeps growing at 4% a year, which is roughly its growth rate since it acquired Reynolds.

At that time, the company invested heavily in NGPs and repaid the debt from the acquisition, which was a drag on growth rates. So, my 4% growth target going forward is slightly conservative, but I tend to base my investment decisions on cautious assumptions, which have served me well over the years.

6.14% dividend yield + 4% growth = 10.14% total return

I believe BTI shares are still slightly undervalued, therefore further multiple expansion is likely over the next decade. But if we conservatively assume neither multiple expansion nor compression going forward, BTI is expected to yield double-digit total returns.

It could be considered an inflation hedge, as demand for nicotine is relatively inelastic even during periods of heightened inflation, and the high gross margins offer a buffer if costs were to rise.

At current valuations, there's a heightened chance that BTI might outperform the S&P 500 over the medium term, which I view as the next decade.

The S&P 500 is trading at a 39.60 CAPE ratio, which is in the upper range of historical valuations. Given the high valuations, its real returns are expected to be in the low-single digits over the next 10 to 15 years. I believe BTI could crush it.

If someone asks me if growth stocks or income stocks are better, my answer is simple: it depends on the valuation. A company is worth its future FCF to the present, and our goal is to receive as much future FCF at the lowest price possible. Even a great company can be a bad investment, if bought at the wrong price.

BTI is still trading at the right price, but of course it comes with single-company risks, which we're going to cover now, as we're going to focus on new developments in the NGP categories.

New Category Launches

One of the biggest risks for long-term tobacco investors is the transition to NGPs, as it opened the door to new competitors. But the shift also comes with opportunities, as the new products are believed to be less risky than cigarettes, and margins tend to be even higher than traditional combustibles due to lower government taxes.

On its Capital Markets Day last year, the company introduced new products, and all of these have started to be launched in some markets recently.

As the largest position in my portfolio, I would like to report only great news and get you pumped up, but that's the wrong approach.

I'll try to report the latest developments objectively, so we can get a feel for where the NGP portfolio is headed over the next year and make realistic assumptions for the company's trajectory.

Keep in mind, however, that BTI still generates the vast majority of its profits with its combustible segment. The market is overly focused on NGPs, but the developments over the next year likely don't put the cash flows nor the dividend in danger.

glo Hilo

In one of my previous articles, I was disappointed with the slow glo Hilo rollout. Back then, BTI only released the two devices in Serbia and the less-premium model in one region in Japan.

That has changed, and now the two-piece premium glo Hilo Plus is available in Japan countrywide, the largest heated tobacco market globally. Both devices are now also available in Poland, one of the markets where glo holds a relatively high market share of 35.1%.

Both devices come with the new rivo sticks, which contain less tobacco and therefore should be subject to lower taxes in most countries. The sticks resemble the smaller IQOS sticks. Less tobacco content and lower taxes equal higher profit per stick for BTI, as long as sales won't disappoint.

BTI sells the premium glo Hilo Plus for JPY6,890 (USD46.41) in Japan, and PLN289 (USD79.10) in Poland. In both cases, those are prices that are similar to the premium IQOS devices.

The entry-level glo Hilo model sells for roughly half the price, but compared to previous-generation glo models (which were often given away for almost free), that's a steep price tag.

Not only did the company raise prices on the devices, but more importantly on the new sticks, as the old ones can't be used with the new devices.

Rivo sticks cost PLN19.99, which is a 28% price increase compared to the previous Neo sticks. In Japan, BTI also raised prices to IQOS levels.

Now, in order for consumers to switch to the glo Hilo (and accept IQOS-like prices) the new devices have to deliver a great performance when it comes to taste and usability.

In my previous article on the entry-level Hilo device, I was disappointed with the early data. The device heats up in just 5 seconds, which is four times faster than previous generations and IQOS. But because of the fast heat-up, the first few draws taste burnt. That's unacceptable for most users.

In the recently launched glo Hilo Plus, the time was extended to 9 seconds. The first few draws still taste slightly burnt, but less than before. After the first few seconds, Hilo Plus starts to taste more similar to IQOS.

Now, glo doesn't have the brand equity of IQOS. The Philip Morris brand can be compared to Apple in some ways, users are willing to pay more for the perceived premium product.

In order for BTI to compete, it has to deliver a vastly better experience, especially if both are selling at the same price point. glo Hilo is lightweight, has a great display, faster heat-up, and longer smoking time compared to the competition. The flavor is now more similar to IQOS, which I view as a plus.

With the limited data so far, a lot of users have complained about the high price tag of the devices, as well as the sticks. The main selling point for previous glo generations was the affordability.

How many users will switch remains to be seen. I expect the previous generation to be discontinued, as BTI wants to premiumize glo in order to achieve decent profitability.

All in all, glo Hilo won't likely be the breakthrough that will put a dent in IQOS sales, but the brand is heading in the right direction, and in some ways are already leading the way. I expect revenues and profitability to improve, as glo is becoming a premium brand in the years ahead. Patience is required when it comes to heated tobacco, the most profitable new category.

Vuse Ultra

Another device that has been launched in markets like Germany, the UK, and other European countries is Vuse Ultra. It's a smart vaping device, where users can customize their experience via the built-in display or the app.

The device comes with new pods, that come with a higher price tag. Here in Germany, the Vuse Ultra pods (2-piece) cost EUR13.95, or 16% more than the regular ones. The device has been selling for a couple of weeks already, therefore I went to several gas stations and convenience stores to ask if it's selling well. In all cases, I was told the regular ones were selling better, but all sales were tiny compared to Chinese competitors.

I then headed to Amazon and checked the data there. The Vuse Ultra sold 50 pieces last month, while the regular Vuse Pro One sold 200 devices. To put it mildly, the new Vuse Ultra hasn't gone viral here in Germany.

There are only a few promotional videos on the internet, no user reviews. I guess it's also not selling well in the rest of Europe, unfortunately.

The vaping category is a weak point for BTI, as revenues have been declining due to illicit Chinese competition, which seems to offer better value to consumers.

A recent visit to my local Aldi has been eye-opening. They used to sell Vuse vapes exclusively, but now only sell Elfbars, the best-selling Chinese competitor.

Compared to the Chinese competition, BTI doesn't seem to have a chance. In order for the category to grow, we need to see further enforcement in its largest market, the U.S.

Velo Shift

The company just introduced Velo Shift in Sweden, a relevant market for nicotine pouches. The main selling point is its new shape, which is marketed as "comfort shape".

The product has been launched only recently, so there's limited data. On social media, people doubt that it's as comfortable as marketed, but they haven't used it yet.

To me, the shape looks great. If it's soft and comfortable, it's a decent upgrade. Innovation is important to stay the market leader in Europe, and BTI delivers it. Well done.

Velo Plus

In the U.S., Velo Plus continues its rapid rise. As mentioned in earlier articles, investors in Philip Morris (and that includes me) should be concerned, as I believe Velo Plus is the better product compared to ZYN. Given the endless reviews I've read so far, the majority of users seem to agree with me.

In the last half-year report, volume share of Velo in the U.S. almost doubled and reached 13.2%. In July, it reached another record with more than 17% share. While I don't have more recent data, I believe Velo Plus is still gaining market share. It's the most promising product and category in BTI's NGP portfolio.

The FDA started a nicotine pouch pilot to ease manufacturers' research burden. It aims to fast-track nicotine pouch reviews, a step in the right direction.

Robert F. Kennedy Jr. calls nicotine pouches the safest way to consume nicotine, and I agree with him. The stars seem to align for the nicotine pouch industry in the U.S., and BTI has launched a great product at the right time with Velo Plus.

Conclusion

To sum things up, British American Tobacco continues to be one of the more interesting opportunities in today's overvalued markets—not because of rapid growth, but because of reliable cash flows and reasonable valuations.

The transition to NGPs isn't smooth across the board, but it is moving in the right direction overall. Glo is improving, Vuse still has room for improvement, and Velo Plus might end up being the company's breakout product in the U.S.

At this stage, I'm not expecting fireworks—and frankly, I don't need them. The core business remains intact, the growing dividend seems safe, and there's decent optionality in the NGP segment in the years ahead. For now, I'm collecting the dividends and watching how the rollout of the new products plays out. As always, valuation and fundamentals will determine my outlook—not headlines.

data.title: GGN: Attractive For Income But Faces Energy Sector Risks
data.description:
GAMCO Global Gold, Natural Resources & Income Trust (NYSE:GGN), a closed-end fund, managed by Gabelli, invests in energy and precious metal stocks, adding covered calls. The market price tends to follow the posted asset value most recently at $5.0. Over long periods of time, this investment hasn't been stellar, but during periods of more inflationary environments, this is almost a must. We, for a significant period of time, owned a sizable lot, receiving the nice $0.03 per month dividend. Oil/Energy

This fund also invests in energy companies such as Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX). An article in OilPrice noted that the IEA, a wayward organization, "is about to revise its peak oil demand scenario radically..." In the past, this organization carried water for the global elite, panning climate change, CO₂, and energy, claiming that the sky is falling. IEA was the rogue actor. Apparently, a change of allegiance prompted this radical departure. Crude oil and other fossil fuels aren't peaking in demand anytime soon. OPEC+ and IEA publicly clashed often over the past several years. Reality set in, forcing the change of mind. With more common certainty of crude demand, the issue becomes with whom. Reuters noted that "growth was coming from non-OPEC countries in North and South America, notably Canada, Guyana, Brazil, and Argentina, as well as Suriname." Interestingly noted, the U.S. is missing from future growth; focusing the fund investments must have a measured exposure to other Western Hemisphere countries. And Reuters also noted that growth will have political consequences.

Volume growth, at some level, isn't the issue. The issue remains prices driven by supply/demand balances. On the issue, the news seems split, but the consensus for now points to glut. Again, from OilPrice, we found this,

Goldman Sachs expects the global oil market to swing into a surplus of 1.9 million barrels daily next year as a result of OPEC+’s output cut unwinding and producers in North and South America also ramping up production.

There is a very strong argument for mid-$50s priced crude coming perhaps next year. This won't bode well for production-based energy investments.

Reviewing Risks and Rewards with Short Option Investments

The strategy of writing covered calls provides cash for paying dividends. It also adds risk, especially with stocks that run significantly past strike prices. With gold climbing through the roof, a review of a couple of gold-driven stocks seems in order. We choose Newmont (NEM) and Barrick (B). For Newmont, the latest reports show approximately 600,000 shares with calls at the mid-$60s expiring between October and December. The stock price ran through $65 several weeks ago and is now trading at the mid-$80s.

A review of Barrick illustrates a similar path. The fund holds just less than 600,000 shares, with most of the shares covered with short calls ranging between $21-$25 expiring between July and November. The current price is $29, significantly higher than the strike prices.

Reviewing a couple of energy stocks now seems in order, beginning with Exxon. At the end of June, the fund held approximately 450,000 shares. Short calls written against ranged in price from $110 to $120 with expirations between July and January of 2026. Interestingly, the lower strike prices expire in the later part of 2025 and early 2026. There is a natural cyclical period in crude pricing; thus, the models expect lower stock pricing in winter months. The current price is approximately $115.

Continuing, the fund holds approximately 175,000 shares of Chevron. The strike prices were between $150 and $160, with the lower-priced strikes expiring in the January 26 timeframe. The closing price on the 19th of September was approximately $155, again in the ranges of the strike prices.

The precious metal investments have seemingly run above the strikes reported at the end of June. The energy sector provides cash from written short calls. The fund pays out $0.03 per month, or approximately $5 million in dividends. There are also expenses.

What To Watch

In closed funds, asset growth comes through internal growth. When short call strategies are used and prices run ahead of the strikes, long-term asset damage can occur. Managers have choices. During one period earlier in the year, the fund lost a significant portion of Newmont but found a soft price period for repurchasing. This is one lever. A second lever uses longer option dates to roll the current options higher without incurring loss of stock. This eats into cash generation. The cash/bond positions pay approximately $5 million per year in interest, or one month's worth. The balance must come through short calls or dividends.

The next quarterly report will show investors a lot about what approaches management chose to cover the cash outflows. Again, the risk equals losing long-term capital through ending up with fewer shares of individual stocks. When gold heads south, this matters.

Risk & Investment

In our view, the risk resides with energy going forward, at least through the balance of the year. Crude oil, thus the energy holdings, tends to follow cyclical patterns, being weak in the winter months. We have read estimates of $50 per barrel pricing or something close this year. The fund's energy holdings will suffer. We also expect precious metals to continue higher with the risk that stock prices might continue to run ahead of strikes. In this environment, holding the asset size, requires strategies that either cost cash generated elsewhere, or shunt cash generation. The September report will shed a lot of light on this issue.

But the asset value has been growing with metal prices and its respective value. The above TradeStation chart strongly suggests this outcome.
